does: This paper introduces OpenAI Microscope, a public visualization tool that systematically examines every major layer and neuron across eight canonical computer vision models used in interpretability research. The tool generates detailed visualizations of learned features at each layer, revealing what individual neurons and feature maps detect through activation maximization and other analysis techniques. Rather than proposing a novel algorithm, this work provides an infrastructure resource that makes feature inspection more accessible and scalable, enabling researchers to directly observe and analyze the internal representations that form across different architectures and training regimes without requiring specialized code or deep technical expertise.
connects: This effort builds on a rich tradition of neural network interpretability work, including foundational visualization research showing that convolutional networks learn hierarchical features and visualization-based investigations of what hidden units represent. The work responds to the challenge identified by earlier interpretability research that understanding large networks remains difficult despite decades of investigation, and complements mechanistic interpretability approaches that aim to reverse-engineer neural computations. Microscope extends prior one-off visualization studies by systematizing the process across multiple architectures and layers, following the principle established in neuroscience and adopted in ML that studying standardized "model organisms" accelerates cumulative understanding.
enables: By providing free, organized access to layer-by-layer visualizations, Microscope lowers barriers for researchers exploring feature hierarchies and enables systematic comparative studies of different architectures and training procedures. The tool facilitates discovery of which features emerge reliably across models versus which are architecture-specific, supporting the development of more complete theories of deep learning. Future work can build on these visualizations to develop automated feature classification schemes, investigate how features relate to model robustness and generalization, and connect interpretability findings to mechanistic explanations of model behavior, ultimately advancing the practical utility of interpretability for neural network safety and alignment.
